ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for braised pig large intestine; closest parent 'pork intestine food product' used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers pig large intestine as a raw ingredient category; braised preparation not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for braised pig large intestine or pork intestine dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of braised pig large intestine is approximately 150 g, served as a side dish or accompaniment in a Bak Kut Teh or braised pork meal.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, large intestine, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 10397)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy data for cooked pork large intestine used as the primary nutrient base. Values adjusted to reflect braised preparation with soy sauce and aromatics: sodium is elevated due to soy sauce braising liquid absorption. Carbohydrate and sugar values reflect minor contribution from rock sugar and dark soy sauce in the braising stock. This is the closest approved source match; no direct entry for soy-braised pig large intestine exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein- and fat-dominant foods — analogous estimate based on cooked pork offal and meat-dominant dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised pig large intestine. GI estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database convention for high-fat, high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate meat and offal dishes. Carbohydrate content is approximately 1.8 g per 100 g, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 0.4 per 150 g serving. GI category is low. This estimation is consistent with peer-reviewed literature showing that fat- and protein-dominant foods with minimal carbohydrate have GI values in the range of 10–20.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce braising
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid braised pig large intestine due to its high sodium content (approximately 520 mg per 100 g), which can significantly contribute to daily sodium intake and elevate blood pressure; a 150 g serving provides approximately 780 mg sodium, representing over one-third of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content with negligible glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Braised pig large intestine has a very low carbohydrate content of approximately 1.8 g per 100 g and a low glycemic index, making it unlikely to cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the high fat and cholesterol content and consume this dish in moderation as part of a balanced dietary plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat and cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Braised pig large intestine is high in saturated fat (approximately 4.8 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (approximately 150 mg per 100 g), both of which are associated with increased LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular risk; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should consume this dish infrequently and in small portions, and should consider the cumulative effect of the high sodium content on blood pressure and cardiac load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised pig large intestine (卤猪大肠; Lu Zhu Da Chang) is a traditional Chinese and Malaysian-Singaporean dish in which the cleaned large intestine of the pig is slow-braised in a fragrant soy sauce stock seasoned with dark soy sauce, five-spice powder, star anise, cinnamon, garlic, ginger, and rock sugar. The braising process renders the intestine tender and deeply flavoured, with a characteristic gelatinous, slightly chewy texture and a rich, savoury-sweet umami taste. The dish is commonly served as part of a Bak Kut Teh meal, as a braised offal side dish, or alongside rice and other braised meats. Nutritionally, braised pig large intestine is a high-fat, moderate-protein, very-low-carbohydrate food. Per 100 g it provides approximately 168 kcal, 9.8 g protein, 13.5 g fat (of which 4.8 g is saturated), and only 1.8 g carbohydrate. The dish is notably high in cholesterol (approximately 150 mg per 100 g) and sodium (approximately 520 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce absorption during braising). Its glycemic index is low (estimated at 15) and its glycemic load per serving is negligible. Clinically, this dish is of concern for individuals with hypertension due to its high sodium content, and for those with cardiovascular disease due to its saturated fat and cholesterol load. It is not a significant concern for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes given its minimal carbohydrate content, but overall dietary moderation is advised. The dish is not halal and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for braised pig large intestine was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked pork large intestine (NDB 10397) with adjustments for soy sauce braising. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database conventions for high-fat, high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate meat dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the USDA source is a high-quality approved source and the adaptation rationale is well-documented.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked pork large intestine, adjusted for soy sauce braising. Sodium is elevated to 520 mg per 100 g to reflect soy sauce absorption during braising; the USDA base value for plain cooked pork large intestine is approximately 60–80 mg per 100 g. 2. Carbohydrate_g (1.8 g) and sugar_g (0.8 g) reflect minor contributions from rock sugar and dark soy sauce in the braising stock; these are not naturally zero but are very low. 3. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as pork intestine contains no dietary fibre; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 4. Vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 as cooked pork offal contains negligible vitamin C; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 5. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this preparation in the source database. 6. Folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported for this preparation in the source database. 7. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported for this preparation in the source database. 8. Vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported for this preparation in the source database. 9. Selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported for this preparation in the source database. 10. GI is estimated at 15 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database conventions for high-fat, high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate meat and offal dishes; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. GL per 150 g serving = (15 × 1.8 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.405, rounded to 0.4. 11. The dish is not halal as it contains pork. 12. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for braised pig large intestine as a prepared dish.
